Identity.History: Darius I, called Darius the Great, ruled the Achaemenid Persian Empire from 522–486 BC. After seizing power during a succession crisis, he defeated rebellions and reorganized the empire into satrapies governed by royal officials. He improved roads, taxation, coinage, administration, and monumental building, including work at Persepolis. His empire stretched from the Indus Valley to Egypt and Anatolia. Darius also expanded into Thrace and Scythia, but his invasion of Greece ended in defeat at Marathon in 490 BC.
